section.title: The Fool Says There Is No God
section.sectionRationale: A near twin of Psalm 14, revised for Book II of the Psalter with the divine name Elohim rather than YHWH throughout. It diagnoses universal human corruption, all have turned away, and closes with a longing for salvation to come out of Zion, a passage Paul later quotes in Romans 3 to prove that every human being needs the righteousness only found in Christ.
section.thesis: Universal human corruption, everyone has turned away, leaves no one able to seek God on their own; the psalm's ache for a Savior out of Zion is answered in Christ, whom Paul quotes this very psalm to prove all people need.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the fool's denial of God and the resulting universal corruption; the folly of the workers of iniquity who devour God's people yet will be overwhelmed with dread; and the closing longing for salvation to come out of Zion.

units.label: The fool says in his heart, there is no God

verses.text:
The {{nabal|fool}} says in his heart, “There is no God.” 
They are corrupt; their ways are vile. There is no one who does good.
God looks down from heaven upon the sons of men to see if any understand, if any seek God.
All have turned away, they have together become corrupt; there is no one who does good, not even one.

units.label: Will the workers of iniquity never learn
units.note: the folly of oppressing God's people

verses.text:
Will the workers of iniquity never learn? 
They devour my people like bread; they refuse to call upon God.
There they are, overwhelmed with dread, where there was nothing to fear. For God has scattered the bones of those who besieged you. You put them to shame, for God has despised them.

units.label: Oh, that salvation would come from Zion

verses.text:
Oh, that the salvation of Israel would come from Zion! 
When God {{shuv|restores}} His captive people, let Jacob rejoice, let Israel be glad!

nabal.gloss: Fool, but not primarily a matter of low intelligence; it describes moral perversity, a practical, willful denial of God despite the evidence of His existence and character. The nabal's problem is a corrupt heart, not a slow mind.

shuv.gloss: To turn, to return, to restore. The same root describes humanity's universal turning away from God in verse 3 and God's promised turning again, restoring His captive people, in verse 6, a wordplay linking the fall and the hoped-for reversal.

basic.context: This psalm is nearly identical to Psalm 14, revised here for a collection that prefers the name Elohim, God, over the personal covenant name YHWH, the LORD. It diagnoses the human condition with unflinching honesty: everyone has turned away, not one does good. The wicked devour God's people without a thought of calling on Him, yet the psalm closes not in despair but in longing for the salvation that will come out of Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm is not merely a description of especially bad people; it is a diagnosis of universal human nature apart from God's grace, which Paul later cites as proof that every person, not just the obviously wicked, stands guilty before God and needs a righteousness that is not their own.
basic.originalLanguages: The nabal, fool, denies God not from ignorance but from moral corruption of heart. The same Hebrew root, shuv, describes both humanity's universal turning away from God, verse 3, and God's future turning again to restore His people, verse 6, a deliberate echo tying the problem and its promised solution together.
basic.typology: The psalm's closing longing for salvation to come out of Zion anticipates the salvation that did, in fact, come from Zion in the person of Jesus Christ, born of David's line, whose gospel alone answers the universal corruption this psalm exposes.
basic.christ: Paul quotes this psalm's opening verses directly in Romans 3:10-12 as biblical proof that all people, Jew and Gentile alike, are under sin, building the case for the gospel: since none are righteous, none seek God, righteousness must come as a gift through faith in Jesus Christ, Romans 3:21-26. The psalm's longing, oh that salvation would come from Zion, is answered literally and fully in Christ.
basic.god: God is shown searching all humanity from heaven and finding universal corruption, yet He is also the one who will restore His captive people and bring salvation out of Zion, showing both His perfect knowledge of sin and His unfailing commitment to redeem.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:10-12 quotes this psalm directly to prove universal human sinfulness. Romans 3:21-26 shows God's righteousness given by faith in Jesus Christ as the answer to that universal need. Luke 2:11 announces that a Savior has been born in the city of David, the literal fulfillment of salvation coming out of Zion.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's stark diagnosis, no one seeks God, no one does good, leaves humanity with nowhere to turn but grace. Its longing for salvation out of Zion is answered in Jesus Christ, born in David's city, in whom alone the righteousness this psalm shows no one possesses is freely given to all who believe.

context.literary: A wisdom and lament psalm nearly identical to Psalm 14, belonging to the Elohistic Psalter, Psalms 42-83, which consistently substitutes Elohim, God, for the personal covenant name YHWH found in parallel texts elsewhere. Its three movements move from diagnosis, verses 1-3, to the folly and coming dread of the wicked, verses 4-5, to a closing longing for salvation, verse 6.
context.historical: Likely composed or adapted for use in a specific historical crisis in which God's people faced siege or oppression, verse 5's reference to besiegers scattered suggesting a particular deliverance, though the psalm's central diagnosis applies to every generation.
context.canonical: Quoted directly by Paul in Romans 3:10-12 as foundational proof of universal human sinfulness, making this psalm, alongside its twin Psalm 14, one of the most theologically significant texts behind the doctrine of total depravity and the necessity of the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the universal corruption of humanity apart from God, showing that not even one person truly seeks or does good on their own, while still holding out hope for God's promised salvation and restoration.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the universal condition of humanity honestly and without exception; it does not prescribe despair but points toward the hoped-for salvation that answers this diagnosis.

debates.question: Why does Psalm 53 use Elohim, God, throughout, while its near twin Psalm 14 uses YHWH, the LORD?

views.label: A stylistic feature of the Elohistic Psalter with no major doctrinal difference
views.case: Psalms 42 to 83 as a collection consistently prefer Elohim over YHWH, likely reflecting a particular editorial or liturgical tradition, without changing the psalm's meaning or theology.

views.label: An intentional emphasis on God's universal sovereignty fitting the psalm's message
views.case: Using the more general term for God, rather than Israel's covenant name, may fit a psalm whose message, universal human corruption and God's searching gaze over all mankind, is deliberately broader than Israel's covenant relationship alone.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm's clear teaching, universal sin and humanity's need for God's own salvation, is unaffected by which divine name is used.

study.originalLanguages: The nabal, fool, denies God not from lack of evidence but from a corrupt heart, a moral rather than intellectual failure. The Hebrew root shuv links humanity's universal turning away from God, verse 3, with God's promised turning again to restore His people, verse 6, tying the psalm's diagnosis to its hoped-for cure.

typology.type: The longing for salvation to come out of Zion, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, born of David's line, in whom salvation came literally and fully out of Zion, Luke 2:11, Romans 11:26-27.

study.christ: Paul quotes this psalm's opening verses directly, Romans 3:10-12, as scriptural proof that all people, Jew and Gentile alike, are under sin, none righteous, none seeking God. This diagnosis drives Paul's argument toward the gospel: since no one can produce their own righteousness, God gives it as a gift through faith in Jesus Christ, Romans 3:21-26. The psalm's closing ache, oh that salvation would come from Zion, is answered literally in the Savior born in David's city and fully in the salvation He accomplished.
study.god: God searches all humanity from heaven and finds universal corruption, yet He remains committed to restore His captive people and bring salvation out of Zion, holding together perfect knowledge of sin with unfailing redemptive purpose.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes this psalm directly to establish the universal sinfulness of all humanity before God.
God's righteousness given by faith in Jesus Christ answers the universal need this psalm exposes.
The literal announcement that a Savior has been born in the city of David, salvation coming out of Zion.

study.oneStory: This psalm's unflinching diagnosis, no one seeks God, no one does good, leaves humanity with nowhere to turn but grace. Its longing for salvation out of Zion is answered fully in Jesus Christ, born in David's city, in whom the righteousness this psalm shows no one possesses is freely given to all who believe.

quiz.q: What does the Hebrew word for 'fool', nabal, primarily describe?

options.text: Moral perversity and willful denial of God, not low intelligence.
options.note: The nabal's failure is one of heart and will, denying God despite evidence, not a lack of mental ability.

options.text: A person with a diagnosed learning disability.
options.note: The term is not describing intellectual capacity at all; it describes moral corruption.

options.text: Someone who has simply never heard about God.
options.note: The psalm treats the denial as willful, in his heart, not as innocent ignorance from lack of exposure.

options.text: A person who lacks formal education.
options.note: The psalm nowhere ties this to education; it describes a heart posture of denying God, often even among the intellectually accomplished.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's diagnosis of humanity in verses 1-3?

options.text: All have turned away and become corrupt; there is no one who does good, not even one.
options.note: This is the psalm's unflinching, universal diagnosis, stated twice for emphasis.

options.text: Most people are good, but a small minority are corrupt.
options.note: The psalm explicitly denies this, saying there is no one who does good, not even one.

options.text: Only foreign nations are corrupt; Israel is entirely righteous.
options.note: The psalm's diagnosis is universal, applying to humanity broadly, not selectively to other nations alone.

options.text: People are born neutral and become corrupt only through poor choices later in life.
options.note: The psalm describes a present, universal corruption without specifying its precise origin in this way; its focus is the universal fact, not a developmental theory.

quiz.q: How does the apostle Paul use this psalm in Romans 3?

options.text: He quotes it directly as scriptural proof that all people, Jew and Gentile alike, are under sin.
options.note: Romans 3:10-12 quotes this psalm's opening verses almost verbatim to establish universal human sinfulness.

options.text: He argues that this psalm applies only to Gentiles, not to Jews.
options.note: Paul's whole argument in Romans 3 is that both Jews and Gentiles alike are under sin; this psalm proves the universal point.

options.text: He dismisses this psalm as no longer relevant under the new covenant.
options.note: Paul treats this psalm as authoritative Scripture, directly quoting it to build his argument, not dismissing it.

options.text: He uses it to argue that good works alone can still save a person.
options.note: Paul uses this psalm to argue precisely the opposite, that no one can be justified by works since none do good.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing longing in verse 6?

options.text: That the salvation of Israel would come from Zion, and God would restore His captive people.
options.note: This is the psalm's climactic hope, stated explicitly in the final verse.

options.text: That Israel's military would grow strong enough to conquer its enemies alone.
options.note: The hope expressed is for God's salvation and restoration, not for Israel's independent military strength.

options.text: That the fool would simply change his own mind through better arguments.
options.note: The psalm's hope is in God's saving action, not in the fool's self-persuasion.

options.text: That the temple would be rebuilt with greater architectural splendor.
options.note: The psalm's longing is for salvation and restoration of God's people, not for physical construction.

quiz.q: How is this psalm's closing longing, 'salvation would come from Zion,' fulfilled?

options.text: Literally and fully in Jesus Christ, the Savior born in David's city of Zion.
options.note: Luke 2:11 announces the birth of the Savior in the city of David, the literal answer to this psalm's ache.

options.text: It remains entirely unfulfilled and without any answer in Scripture.
options.note: The New Testament presents Christ as the direct fulfillment of exactly this kind of longing.

options.text: Only through the political restoration of the modern state of Israel.
options.note: Scripture's own fulfillment language points to the person and work of Christ, not merely to modern political events.

options.text: Only in a future, entirely separate salvation unrelated to Jesus.
options.note: The New Testament consistently ties Old Testament hopes for salvation directly to the person of Jesus Christ, not a separate, unrelated fulfillment.

questions: This psalm says the fool's problem is a corrupt heart, not lack of evidence for God. Where do you see yourself, or people around you, denying truths about God not from ignorance but from unwillingness to submit to Him?
The psalm's diagnosis is universal: no one does good, not even one. How does resisting the temptation to see yourself as an exception to this diagnosis change the way you read the rest of the psalm?

Paul uses this exact psalm to argue that righteousness must come as a gift through faith in Christ, since no one can produce their own. Where are you still relying on your own goodness rather than receiving God's gift of righteousness?

The psalm ends not in despair but in longing for salvation to come from Zion. What are you currently longing for God to do, in your own life or in the world, and how does knowing salvation has already come in Christ shape that longing?
The wicked in this psalm devour God's people without a thought of calling on Him, yet the psalm still expects God to act. Where do you need to trust God's justice for wrongs you cannot fix yourself?

translationNotes.note: The fool says in his heart, there is no God more precisely conveys a practical, willful denial or dismissal of God, not primarily a formal, philosophical claim of atheism, since the whole psalm is concerned with moral corruption flowing from this posture.

This psalm closely parallels Psalm 14 but consistently substitutes Elohim, God, for YHWH, the LORD, reflecting its place within the Elohistic Psalter, Psalms 42-83, and it varies slightly from Psalm 14 in verse 5, describing dread and shame in place of Psalm 14's language about the LORD's presence with the righteous.

When God restores His captive people renders a Hebrew phrase that can also be understood as a reference to the return from exile, though its ultimate answer, as Paul's use of the psalm's opening verses shows, is the salvation accomplished through Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 53:1-6. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) confirmed for Psalm 53 this session; sermon status set to none.
